skill.name: seedance-v2
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to generate short cinematic videos with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro through the RunComfy CLI and Model API.
skill.summary: Generate short Seedance 2.0 Pro videos through RunComfy with prompt, reference media, and CLI guidance.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are Markdown examples, placeholder URLs, or text matches rather than executable code. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt injection in SKILL.md. The skill still needs a marketplace warning because it instructs users to run a local CLI that sends prompts and reference media URLs to RunComfy and writes outputs or tokens locally.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Invocation Sends User Data to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run the RunComfy CLI with prompt input and reference media. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it sends user content to an external service and depends on a local executable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands and API description clearly show user prompt data being submitted through RunComfy. This appears intentional and legitimate, but it is still an external data transfer risk.

medium_findings.title: Reference Media URLs Are Processed Outside the User Machine
medium_findings.description: The skill supports image, video, and audio URLs as model inputs and states that external URLs are fetched by the RunComfy model server. Users may expose private media URLs if they provide sensitive references.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The schema examples and privacy note explicitly describe remote processing of reference URLs. The behavior is appropriate for a hosted video model but requires user awareness.

medium_findings.title: Local Token and Output File Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents token storage under a user configuration path and writes generated assets to a selected output directory. This is normal CLI behavior, but shared machines and broad output paths increase exposure risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and token storage behavior are documented directly in the skill. The stated mode 0600 reduces risk, so this is a moderate operational concern rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby shell execution alerts come from Markdown inline code, tables, fenced bash examples, and prompt examples. SKILL.md does not contain executable Ruby code or a script body that runs these backticks automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms the detected backticks are Markdown formatting and command examples. No evidence found that the skill executes Ruby backticks.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Placeholder URL False Positives
low_findings.description: The high path traversal alerts point to example URLs containing ellipses, such as media placeholders. These are not filesystem traversal sequences and do not instruct access to parent directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside HTTPS placeholder examples for reference media. No evidence found of ../ path traversal or local file reads in those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections map to prose, headings, prompt guidance, or an exit-code section. No hash function, cipher, or cryptographic implementation is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the flagged lines found descriptive documentation only. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, weak ciphers, or cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Routing and Media-Spec Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to model routing guidance and reference media constraints. They do not collect host information, enumerate files, or inspect the runtime environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are natural-language instructions for choosing the model and validating media duration. No evidence found of system probing commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells the assistant to call a local CLI command. This is not malicious by itself, but users should review inputs, output paths, and the installed RunComfy CLI before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated in the documentation. The risk is operational because the command is expected for the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Network Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow submits model requests to RunComfy and downloads generated assets from RunComfy domains. This should be disclosed because user prompts and media references leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and outbound download behavior are stated directly in the security section. No unrelated telemetry or callback behavior was found.

content.value_statement: Short video workflows need clear model settings and reference-media rules. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to call Seedance 2.0 Pro through RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to choose Seedance 2.0 Pro instead of HappyHorse, Wan, Kling, or LTX models.
Provides RunComfy CLI invocation patterns for text-only, lip-synced, and multimodal video jobs.
Documents accepted inputs, including prompt, image_url, video_url, audio_url, duration, aspect ratio, resolution, audio, and seed.
Gives prompt guidance for stable identity, camera motion, audio tone, ambience, and reference media use.
Lists prerequisites, exit codes, output handling, token storage, and outbound RunComfy endpoints.
limitations: The endpoint is limited to 4 to 15 second videos.
The documented playground variant has a 720p resolution ceiling.
Reference videos and audio must be 2 to 15 seconds, and audio must be under 15 MB.
Lip-sync quality depends on prompt clarity, reference quality, and model behavior.

use_cases.title: Create Dialogue Ads
use_cases.description: Generate short spokesperson clips with a reference image, spoken message, and synchronized in-pass audio.

use_cases.title: Prototype Cinematic Shots
use_cases.description: Turn shot descriptions, camera movement, and scene references into quick short-form video previews.

use_cases.title: Build Brand Video Variants
use_cases.description: Use identity references, prompt changes, and seed control to test multiple short creative directions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Text Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Seedance 2.0 Pro on RunComfy to create a 5 second 9:16 video. Prompt: [describe scene, action, camera, lighting, and audio mood].

prompt_templates.title: Lip-Synced Character Clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an 8 second lip-synced clip using this character image: [image URL]. The character says: [dialogue]. Use [camera framing] and [lighting style].

prompt_templates.title: Multimodal Reference Shot
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced image, video, and audio references
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Seedance video using [image URL], [video URL], and [audio URL]. Preserve the subject identity, follow the scene motion, and match the voice mood.

prompt_templates.title: Variant Testing Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare three Seedance 2.0 Pro variants for [campaign goal]. Keep the same subject reference, vary camera motion and tone, and recommend duration, aspect ratio, and seed strategy.

output_examples.input: A marketer asks for a short vertical cafe ad with a barista speaking a daily special.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Seedance 2.0 Pro, recommends a 9:16 8 second clip, separates identity into an image reference, and drafts a concise video prompt.

output_examples.input: A creative director provides a subject photo, a room video, and a voice reference.
output_examples.output: The assistant maps each reference to the correct media input, explains duration limits, and prepares a multimodal RunComfy generation request.

output_examples.input: A team wants repeatable tests for different camera moves.
output_examples.output: The assistant keeps the same references and seed plan, then proposes variants for push-in, locked wide, and handheld follow shots.

best_practices: Put stable identity, clothing, logos, and product appearance in image references instead of long text descriptions.
Keep action, emotion, camera movement, lighting, and audio tone in the prompt as direct plain-language instructions.
Use short, valid reference media and choose an output aspect ratio that matches the source material.
anti_patterns: Do not provide private media URLs unless the user accepts third-party processing by RunComfy.
Do not ask for clips longer than 15 seconds on this endpoint.
Do not mix conflicting visual styles or contradictory camera directions in one prompt.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It guides short video generation with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro through the RunComfy CLI and Model API.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow requires the RunComfy CLI and either browser login or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Can it use image, video, and audio references?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented endpoint supports up to 9 images, 3 videos, and 3 audio references within the stated limits.

faq.question: How long can generated clips be?
faq.answer: The documented duration range is 4 to 15 whole seconds.

faq.question: Where does user data go?
faq.answer: Prompts and reference media URLs are submitted to RunComfy. Generated outputs are downloaded from RunComfy domains.

faq.question: Is this skill blocked by the security audit?
faq.answer: No. The audit found no confirmed malicious intent, but publication should show a warning for CLI, network, and filesystem use.
